# `requirements.txt` anatomy

Every `requirements.txt` produced by `pip-compile` follows the same structure. This page walks the
sections so you can read or edit one with confidence.

## Overall layout

```text
# Header
# (autogenerated comment, command line)

# Index options
# (--index-url, --extra-index-url, --find-links, --trusted-host, format controls)

# Pinned packages
# (editable installs first, then alphabetical)

# Unsafe packages
# (only when --allow-unsafe is set, or as commented-out names by default)
```

Each section is independent. `--no-header` strips the first; `--no-emit-options` strips the second;
neither affects the pinned packages.

## The header

```text
#
# This file is autogenerated by pip-compile with Python 3.13
# by the following command:
#
#    pip-compile --output-file=requirements.txt --strip-extras pyproject.toml
#
```

Three pieces of information:

- The Python major.minor version that ran `pip-compile`. Diagnostic only; `pip` will install the file
  under any Python.
- The command line, normalised. Defaults are dropped, one-off flags (`--upgrade`, `--upgrade-package`,
  `--rebuild`, `--quiet`, `--verbose`, `--cache-dir`, `--no-reuse-hashes`, `--no-config`) get stripped,
  options are sorted, secrets in URLs are redacted with `****`.
- A reference to `CUSTOM_COMPILE_COMMAND` if that variable was set.

`--no-header` removes this block.

## Index options

```text
--index-url https://my-index.example.com/simple
--extra-index-url https://pypi.org/simple
--find-links ./vendor/wheels
--trusted-host my-index.example.com
--no-binary :all:
--only-binary numpy
```

The order:

1. `--index-url` (always first if present).
2. `--extra-index-url` lines (one per extra index, deduplicated).
3. `--find-links` lines (one per location, deduplicated).
4. `--trusted-host` lines (one per host, deduplicated).
5. Format controls: `--no-binary :all:` and `--only-binary :all:` first, then per-package
   `--no-binary <pkg>` and `--only-binary <pkg>` lines.

Several flags strip parts of this section:

- `--no-emit-index-url` removes the index URLs.
- `--no-emit-find-links` removes the find-links.
- `--no-emit-trusted-host` removes the trusted hosts.
- `--no-emit-options` removes everything in this section.

## Pinned packages

```text
asgiref==3.7.2
    # via django
django==4.2.7
    # via my-app (pyproject.toml)
sqlparse==0.4.4
    # via django
```

Each entry has:

- The package name, PEP 503 canonicalised (lowercase, hyphens not underscores or dots).
- The pinned version with `==`.
- An optional `; <marker>` if the input had a PEP 508 environment marker.
- An optional `[extras]` if `--strip-extras` is off.
- One or more `--hash=algorithm:digest` lines if `--generate-hashes` is on.
- A `# via <source>` annotation, unless `--no-annotate` is set.

Sort order: editable installs first, then alphabetical by canonical name.

## Annotations

The `# via` comment names what asked for the package. Two styles:

`split` (default):

```text
asgiref==3.7.2
    # via
    #   django
    #   django-extensions
```

`line`:

```text
asgiref==3.7.2     # via django, django-extensions
```

Single-source annotations are always one line:

```text
django==4.2.7
    # via my-app (pyproject.toml)
```

The source string can be:

- A package name (the package that depends on this one).
- `-r <file>` for a top-level dependency from a `requirements.in`.
- `-c <file>` for a constraints file.
- `<project> (pyproject.toml)` for a top-level dependency from a `[project].dependencies` table.
- `<project> (pyproject.toml::build-system.requires)` for a static build dependency.
- `<project> (pyproject.toml::build-system.backend::wheel)` for a dynamic build dependency.

Multiple sources stack in the annotation; the package got pulled in by all of them.

## Hash lines

With `--generate-hashes`:

```text
django==4.2.7 \
    --hash=sha256:abc123... \
    --hash=sha256:def456...
    # via my-app (pyproject.toml)
```

One `--hash` per artifact. Wheels and sdists each get a hash. Multi-platform wheels mean multiple lines.
The line continuation backslash means `pip` reads them as one logical line.

Unhashable packages get a warning comment:

```text
# WARNING: pip install will require the following package to be hashed.
# Consider using a hashable URL like https://github.com/.../archive/COMMIT.zip
my-pkg @ file:///local/path/my-pkg
```

## Unsafe packages

By default `pip-compile` filters `pip`, `setuptools`, and `distribute` and writes them as comments:

```text
# The following packages are considered to be unsafe in a requirements file:
# pip
# setuptools
```

`pip install -r` ignores comments, so these are informational only. With `--allow-unsafe` the same
packages appear as ordinary pinned lines:

```text
pip==24.0
setuptools==69.5.1
```

In hash mode without `--allow-unsafe`, an extra warning:

```text
# WARNING: The following packages were not pinned, but pip requires them to be
# pinned when the requirements file includes hashes...
```

See {doc}`/explanation/unsafe-packages`.

## Editable installs

```text
-e file:///path/to/my-pkg
    # via -r requirements.in
```

Editable installs do not get pinned. The output preserves them as `-e <link>` and `pip-sync` reinstalls
them on every run. Editables sort first, before regular packages.

```{seealso}
- {doc}`/how-to/customize-output` for the flags that control each section.
- {doc}`/explanation/strip-extras-and-constraints` for what `--strip-extras` removes from the pinned
  lines.
```
